# phq9.online — full content

## /

What Is the PHQ-9? The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) is a nine-item self-administered screening tool designed to detect and measure the severity of depressive symptoms in primary care and community settings. It asks about the frequency of nine core symptoms of major depressive disorder over the previous two weeks, using a four-point scale that ranges from "not at all" to "nearly every day." The nine items map directly onto the diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, making the PHQ-9 one of the most closely aligned screening instruments available. Because it is brief, self-administered, and free to use, the PHQ-9 has become one of the most widely deployed depression screeners in the world. It is used routinely in primary care offices, mental health clinics, hospitals, research studies, and large national health programmes including NHS Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) in England and CANMAT guidelines in Canada. History and Development The PHQ-9 was developed by Drs. Robert L. Spitzer, Janet B. W. Williams, and Kurt Kroenke as part of the Primary Care Evaluation of Mental Disorders project (PRIME-MD). PRIME-MD was a clinician-administered structured interview published in 1994 that systematically screened for several common mental disorders in primary care. The self-report version, the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), was developed to reduce clinician burden while preserving diagnostic accuracy. The PHQ-9 represents the depression module of that broader questionnaire. The landmark validation study was published by Kroenke, Spitzer, and Williams in the Journal of General Internal Medicine in 2001 (doi:10.1046/j.1525-1497.2001.016009606.x). That study enrolled 6,000 patients across eight primary care clinics and compared PHQ-9 scores to independent structured psychiatric interviews conducted by mental health professionals. The PHQ-9 was developed with support from an educational grant provided by Pfizer Inc., but the instrument itself is in the public domain and may be reproduced freely without permission. Psychometric Properties The 2001 validation study found that a PHQ-9 score of 10 or greater had a sensitivity of 88% and a specificity of 88% for detecting major depressive disorder, as determined by structured interview. These properties have been replicated across dozens of independent studies conducted in diverse populations including adolescents, elderly adults, perinatal women, primary care patients, and general community samples. Internal consistency is high, with Cronbach's alpha typically reported between 0.86 and 0.89. Test-retest reliability is also strong, with intraclass correlation coefficients around 0.84 in stable patients. The PHQ-9 has demonstrated good convergent validity, correlating significantly with the Beck Depression Inventory, the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, and other established measures. Scoring and Severity Bands Each of the nine items is scored 0 (not at all), 1 (several days), 2 (more than half the days), or 3 (nearly every day). The total score ranges from 0 to 27. The five severity bands established in the original validation are as follows: 0–4: Minimal or no depression. No specific action is typically indicated, but continued monitoring is reasonable if symptoms have been present or worsening. 5–9: Mild depression. Watchful waiting and patient education about depression are appropriate. Rescreening in four to six weeks is recommended. 10–14: Moderate depression. A treatment plan, including counselling, pharmacotherapy, or both, should be considered. Referral to a mental health professional is appropriate. 15–19: Moderately severe depression. Active treatment is indicated. Close follow-up and referral are strongly recommended. 20–27: Severe depression. Immediate referral to a mental health specialist and consideration of more intensive interventions, including hospitalisation assessment if safety is a concern, is warranted. Item 10 — Functional Impairment The original PHQ questionnaire includes a tenth item that asks how much the problems listed in items 1–9 have made it difficult to do work, take care of things at home, or get along with other people. This item is sometimes included as a functional impairment indicator but is not scored as part of the 0–27 total. A positive functional impairment item alongside a PHQ-9 score of 5 or greater strengthens the clinical case for intervention. For a detailed multi-domain assessment of functional impact, the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) provides a validated alternative. Cross-Cultural Validation The PHQ-9 has been translated into more than 80 languages and validated in diverse cultural and linguistic contexts including Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, Hindi, Portuguese, and many others. While the psychometric properties are generally replicated, some studies have identified minor differential item functioning across cultures, particularly for somatic items such as changes in sleep and appetite. Clinicians should apply culturally sensitive interpretation when using the tool with populations whose primary language and cultural background differ from the original English-speaking US samples. Strengths of the PHQ-9 The PHQ-9 takes approximately two to three minutes to complete and requires no special training to administer. Its brevity makes it practical for busy clinical settings and for longitudinal monitoring of treatment response over time. The scoring algorithm is transparent and can be explained directly to patients, which supports shared decision-making. Its free-to-use public domain status removes barriers that affect other validated instruments. Limitations The PHQ-9 is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test. A high score does not confirm a diagnosis of major depressive disorder, and a low score does not rule one out. The instrument cannot distinguish between depressive episodes caused by different aetiologies, such as bipolar depression, adjustment disorder, or medically induced mood changes. It does not assess manic or hypomanic episodes, so it should not be used alone when bipolar spectrum disorder is a concern. Some somatic symptoms such as sleep disruption, fatigue, and appetite change can be elevated in people with chronic physical illness or pain, which may inflate PHQ-9 scores in those populations. Researchers have proposed cognitive-affective subscales or adjusted cut-points for use in medically ill patients. What to Do With Your Score A score in the minimal range is reassuring but not a guarantee of wellbeing. If you have been feeling persistently low or hopeless even with a low score, that is worth discussing with a doctor or therapist. A score in the mild range suggests it is worth monitoring your symptoms and considering lifestyle factors such as sleep, physical activity, and social support. A score of 10 or above warrants a conversation with a qualified clinician who can properly evaluate your symptoms, rule out other causes, and discuss evidence-based treatment options. If you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, please contact a crisis service immediately. In the UK, call Samaritans on 116 123. In the US, call or text 988. In an immediate emergency, call local emergency services. Common Misconceptions One common misconception is that "passing" a PHQ-9 (scoring below 10) means you do not have depression. Depression exists on a spectrum and causes significant distress at any score level. Another misconception is that a high score alone is sufficient to start medication. Treatment decisions require clinical assessment that goes far beyond any questionnaire, including medical history, risk factors, patient preferences, and differential diagnosis. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The PHQ-9 is a validated screening instrument intended to support clinical decision-making, not to replace it. Your score provides a useful starting point for a conversation with a healthcare professional but cannot, on its own, constitute a clinical diagnosis of any mental health condition.

---

## /about

About phq9.online What This Site Is phq9.online is an independent website that provides free access to validated, public-domain mental health screening instruments. It is designed for individuals who want to check in on their mental health and for clinicians or researchers who need a convenient, accessible version of these tools. The site currently offers seven screeners: the PHQ-9, PHQ-2, GAD-7, CES-D, PROMIS Depression, WSAS, and WHO-5. Methodology Only instruments that are in the public domain are included on this site. No proprietary screeners requiring licensing fees or permissions are used. Each instrument is implemented exactly as specified in the original published validation, including the scoring algorithm, item wording, response options, and severity bands. The source references and DOI links for each instrument are documented in the screener definitions and on the compare page. No modifications are made to item wording or scoring. The instruments are administered in their standard validated form to preserve the validity and reliability properties established in the peer-reviewed literature. Privacy and Data The site does not require any account, registration, or login. No personally identifiable information is collected or stored on any server. If you choose to save your screening history, it is stored exclusively in your browser's localStorage on your own device. You can export or delete this data at any time from the history page . See the privacy policy for full details. How It Is Funded The site is funded through Google AdSense advertising and may accept direct sponsorships from organisations relevant to the site's audience. Instrument selection, scoring, and content editorial decisions are made independently of any funding arrangement. The site will never include instruments that are not in the public domain, and will never recommend specific commercial products or services within clinical content. Source Code The full source code for phq9.online is open and available at github.com/Evoke4350/phq9-online . The code is released under the MIT licence. Contributions, bug reports, and suggestions are welcome via GitHub Issues. Maintainer phq9.online is maintained by nathanib, a private individual with a personal interest in accessible mental health screening. The maintainer is not a licensed healthcare professional and the site does not provide clinical services. For questions or feedback, see the contact page . What This Site Is Not phq9.online is not a clinical service and does not provide medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment of any kind. It is not affiliated with any health authority, pharmaceutical company, or professional body. The maintainer is not a licensed healthcare professional. The site's instruments and informational content are intended to support individuals in understanding their own mental health and having more informed conversations with their clinicians — not to replace those conversations. If you are concerned about your mental health or the mental health of someone close to you, please speak with a qualified healthcare professional. If you are in crisis, visit the crisis resources page for immediate support. Disclaimer The screening tools on this site are provided for informational purposes only and do not constitute medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. See the full disclaimer for details.

---

## /about-phq9

About the PHQ-9 The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) is a nine-item self-administered screening instrument designed to detect and quantify the severity of depressive symptoms in primary care and community settings. It asks respondents to rate the frequency of nine core symptoms of major depressive disorder over the preceding two weeks, using a four-point scale that ranges from "not at all" (0) to "nearly every day" (3). The nine items map directly onto the diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder set out in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, making the PHQ-9 one of the most criterion-aligned screening instruments available. Because it is brief, freely reproducible, and validated across thousands of patients, the PHQ-9 has become one of the most widely deployed depression screeners in the world. It is used routinely in primary care offices, mental health clinics, hospital outpatient departments, research studies, and large national health programmes, including NHS Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) in England and the Canadian Network for Mood and Anxiety Treatments (CANMAT) clinical guidelines. History and Development The PHQ-9 was developed by Drs. Robert L. Spitzer, Janet B. W. Williams, and Kurt Kroenke as part of the Primary Care Evaluation of Mental Disorders (PRIME-MD) project, which began in the early 1990s. PRIME-MD was a clinician-administered structured interview published in 1994 that systematically screened for several common mental disorders in primary care settings. The self-report version — the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) — was created to reduce clinician burden while preserving diagnostic accuracy. The PHQ-9 represents the depression module of that broader questionnaire. The landmark validation study was published by Kroenke, Spitzer, and Williams in the Journal of General Internal Medicine in 2001 (doi: 10.1046/j.1525-1497.2001.016009606.x ). That study enrolled 6,000 patients across eight primary care clinics in the United States and compared PHQ-9 scores to independent structured psychiatric interviews conducted by mental health professionals. The PHQ-9 was developed with support from an educational grant provided by Pfizer Inc. The instrument itself is in the public domain and may be reproduced freely without permission. A subsequent study by Kroenke, Spitzer, Williams, and Löwe published in 2010 in General Hospital Psychiatry (doi: 10.1016/j.genhosppsych.2009.12.003 ) provided an updated meta-analytic review of the PHQ-9's accuracy across 38 validation studies, further cementing its status as the reference standard for self-report depression screening in primary care. Psychometric Properties The 2001 validation study found that a PHQ-9 score of 10 or greater had a sensitivity of 88% and a specificity of 88% for detecting major depressive disorder, as determined by structured clinical interview. The 2010 meta-analysis across 38 studies confirmed these estimates, reporting a pooled sensitivity of 88% (95% CI 83–92%) and specificity of 85% (95% CI 82–88%) at the standard cut-point of 10, across more than 8,000 participants. Internal consistency is high: Cronbach's alpha is typically reported between 0.86 and 0.89 across different populations. Test-retest reliability is strong, with intraclass correlation coefficients around 0.84 in stable patients over a one-week interval. The PHQ-9 shows good convergent validity, correlating significantly with the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II), the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HDRS-17), and the Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS). Factor analyses generally support a single general depression factor, although some studies have identified a two-factor structure distinguishing somatic/neurovegetative symptoms (sleep, appetite, fatigue) from cognitive/affective symptoms (hopelessness, concentration, suicidal ideation). For most clinical screening purposes the unidimensional total score is sufficient. Scoring and Severity Bands Each of the nine items is scored 0 (not at all), 1 (several days), 2 (more than half the days), or 3 (nearly every day). The total score ranges from 0 to 27. The five severity bands established in the original validation and widely reproduced in clinical guidelines are as follows: 0–4: Minimal or no depression. No specific intervention is typically indicated. Continued monitoring is reasonable if the patient has a history of depression or reports symptoms that are worsening. 5–9: Mild depression. Watchful waiting, patient education about depression, and rescreening in four to six weeks are appropriate responses. Lifestyle factors including physical activity, sleep hygiene, and social support are worth discussing. 10–14: Moderate depression. A treatment plan should be considered. Options include counselling, structured psychological therapy (such as cognitive-behavioural therapy), pharmacotherapy, or a combination. Referral to a mental health professional is appropriate. 15–19: Moderately severe depression. Active treatment is indicated. Close clinical follow-up and referral to a mental health specialist are strongly recommended. Safety assessment is important. 20–27: Severe depression. Immediate referral to a mental health specialist is warranted. More intensive interventions, including consideration of hospital assessment if safety is a concern, should be discussed. Alternative cut-points have been proposed for specific populations. A cut-point of 8 improves sensitivity in perinatal populations, while a cut-point of 11 or 12 may reduce false positives in medically ill patients with prominent somatic symptoms. Clinicians should exercise judgment when applying standard thresholds in these contexts. Item 10 — Functional Impairment The original PHQ questionnaire includes a tenth item asking how much the problems described in items 1 through 9 have made it difficult to do work, take care of things at home, or get along with other people. This item is scored separately and is not included in the 0–27 total. A positive functional impairment item alongside a PHQ-9 score of 5 or greater strengthens the clinical case for intervention. For a more detailed multi-domain assessment of functional impact, the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) provides a validated five-item alternative covering work, home management, social leisure, private leisure, and relationships. Cross-Cultural Validation The PHQ-9 has been translated into more than 80 languages and validated in diverse cultural and linguistic contexts including Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, Hindi, Portuguese, Swahili, and many others. A 2012 systematic review by Manea, Gilbody, and McMillan identified 29 independent validation studies and found that the standard cut-point of 10 performed reasonably well across cultures, with sensitivity ranging from 74% to 100% and specificity from 60% to 96% depending on the population and reference standard. Some studies have identified minor differential item functioning across cultures, particularly for somatic items such as sleep disruption, fatigue, and appetite change, which may carry different cultural salience. Clinicians should apply contextually sensitive interpretation when using the PHQ-9 with populations whose cultural and linguistic background differs substantially from the original English-speaking US samples in which the tool was validated. Validated adapted versions exist for specific groups: the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) is often preferred for perinatal populations, and the Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) addresses specific characteristics of late-life depression. However, the PHQ-9 itself has shown acceptable performance in both older adults and perinatal women and remains widely used in those groups. Comparison with Other Depression Scales Several validated self-report depression instruments exist alongside the PHQ-9. Understanding their differences helps clinicians choose the most appropriate tool. PHQ-2: A two-item ultra-brief screen using items 1 and 2 of the PHQ-9 (anhedonia and depressed mood). At a cut-point of 3 or greater, sensitivity is approximately 83% and specificity is 92% for major depressive disorder (Kroenke, Spitzer, Williams, 2003, doi: 10.1097/01.MLR.0000093487.78664.3C ). The PHQ-2 is suitable as an initial gateway screen; a positive result warrants full PHQ-9 administration. CES-D (Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale): A 20-item instrument developed by Radloff at the National Institute of Mental Health in 1977, designed for use in epidemiological research. It covers depressed mood, somatic symptoms, positive affect (reverse-scored), and interpersonal problems. The CES-D is sensitive to subclinical depressive symptoms and is well validated in community samples, but its length makes it less practical for routine clinical screening than the PHQ-9. Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II): A 21-item self-report instrument that is widely used in clinical and research settings. The BDI-II has strong psychometric properties and good sensitivity to treatment change, but it is a proprietary instrument requiring purchase of a licence. Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HDRS/HAM-D): A clinician-administered instrument, not self-report. The 17-item version is the reference standard for measuring treatment response in clinical trials. It requires trained administration and is not practical for routine screening. WHO-5 Wellbeing Index: A five-item questionnaire assessing subjective wellbeing rather than discrete depressive symptoms. Scores below 50 (on a 0–100 scale) are used as a threshold for further assessment. The WHO-5 is particularly useful for monitoring general wellbeing over time and has been proposed as an initial screen for depression in diabetes care guidelines. PROMIS Depression (Short Form 8a): An eight-item instrument developed by the National Institutes of Health as part of the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System. It uses item response theory calibration and T-scores rather than raw sum scores, enabling comparison across populations. The PROMIS Depression bank is in the public domain and is increasingly used in research and integrated health systems. The PHQ-9 compares favourably with all of these instruments for primary care screening. Its advantages include its brevity (nine items), free public-domain status, direct alignment with DSM diagnostic criteria, and a very large validation evidence base. Strengths The PHQ-9 takes approximately two to three minutes to complete and requires no special training to administer or score. Its brevity makes it practical in busy clinical settings and for longitudinal monitoring of treatment response. The scoring algorithm is transparent and can be explained directly to patients, supporting shared decision-making. Its public-domain status removes barriers affecting proprietary instruments such as the BDI-II, and its large validation evidence base means clinicians can interpret scores with confidence across a wide range of settings and populations. Limitations The PHQ-9 is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test. A high score does not confirm a diagnosis of major depressive disorder, and a low score does not rule one out. The instrument cannot distinguish between depressive episodes caused by different aetiologies — bipolar depression, adjustment disorder with depressed mood, grief reactions, or medically induced mood changes will all produce elevated scores. It does not screen for manic or hypomanic episodes and should not be used as the sole instrument when bipolar spectrum disorder is a clinical concern. Somatic symptoms such as sleep disruption, fatigue, and appetite change can be elevated in people with chronic physical illness or significant pain, which may inflate PHQ-9 scores in those populations independently of depressive disorder. Researchers have proposed cognitive-affective subscales or adjusted cut-points for use in medically ill patients, though these alternatives are not yet universally adopted. Self-report measures are also vulnerable to response biases including social desirability and recall limitations. Patients may minimise or amplify symptoms, and a single assessment provides only a cross-sectional snapshot. Repeated assessment over time provides more clinically meaningful information than any single score. What to Do With Your Score A score in the minimal range (0–4) is generally reassuring, but is not a guarantee of wellbeing. If you have been feeling persistently low or hopeless even with a low score, that is worth discussing with a doctor or therapist. A score in the mild range (5–9) suggests it is worth monitoring your symptoms and considering lifestyle factors such as sleep, physical activity, and social support. A score of 10 or above warrants a conversation with a qualified clinician who can properly evaluate your symptoms, rule out other causes, and discuss evidence-based treatment options including psychological therapy and medication. If you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, please seek help immediately. Visit the crisis resources page for helpline numbers in your country. Primary References Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB. The PHQ-9: validity of a brief depression severity measure. J Gen Intern Med. 2001;16(9):606-613. doi: 10.1046/j.1525-1497.2001.016009606.x Spitzer RL, Kroenke K, Williams JB. Validation and utility of a self-report version of PRIME-MD: the PHQ primary care study. JAMA. 1999;282(18):1737-1744. doi: 10.1001/jama.282.18.1737 Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB, Löwe B. The Patient Health Questionnaire somatic, anxiety, and depressive symptom scales: a systematic review. Gen Hosp Psychiatry. 2010;32(4):345-359. doi: 10.1016/j.genhosppsych.2009.12.003 Manea L, Gilbody S, McMillan D. Optimal cut-off score for diagnosing depression with the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9): a meta-analysis. CMAJ. 2012;184(3):E191-196. doi: 10.1503/cmaj.110829 Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB. The Patient Health Questionnaire-2: validity of a two-item depression screener. Med Care. 2003;41(11):1284-1292. doi: 10.1097/01.MLR.0000093487.78664.3C

---

## /ces-d

What Is the CES-D? The Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (CES-D) is a 20-item self-report questionnaire originally designed for use in large-scale epidemiological research on depression in the general population. Unlike the PHQ-9, which was developed primarily for clinical screening in primary care, the CES-D was developed with the explicit aim of measuring the prevalence and distribution of depressive symptoms across community samples. It asks respondents to rate how frequently they experienced each of twenty symptoms during the past week, using a four-point frequency scale ranging from rarely or none of the time (less than one day) to most or all of the time (five to seven days). The CES-D captures six major symptom groups: depressed affect, positive affect, somatic and retarded activity, interpersonal difficulties, and two additional positive-framing items. Its 20-item breadth gives it greater coverage of the depression symptom spectrum than shorter instruments, making it sensitive to subclinical levels of distress as well as more severe presentations. This breadth has made it one of the most cited depression scales in the epidemiological literature. History and Development The CES-D was developed by Lenore Sawyer Radloff at the Center for Epidemiologic Studies, National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH), United States. It was first described in a paper published in Applied Psychological Measurement in 1977 (doi:10.1177/014662167700100306). Radloff assembled the scale by drawing items from previously validated depression measures including the Beck Depression Inventory, the Zung Self-Rating Depression Scale, the MMPI Depression Scale, and the Raskin Depression Rating Scale. Items were selected to represent the major components of the depressive syndrome that could be assessed through self-report and that were appropriate for community use. The instrument was designed as a public domain tool to facilitate large-scale community surveys without the licensing barriers that might restrict epidemiological research. Its development was funded through NIMH and it remains freely available. The original 1977 paper reported results from two community surveys and a sample of psychiatric patients, establishing the basic psychometric properties and scoring conventions that are still in use today. Psychometric Properties The original validation reported by Radloff found internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) of 0.85 in community samples and 0.90 in a psychiatric patient sample. Test-retest reliability over one year was approximately 0.59, reflecting both genuine symptom change over time and some measurement instability. Subsequent studies have consistently reported alpha values in the range of 0.84 to 0.90 across diverse samples. Using the conventional cut-point of 16 or greater, the CES-D has demonstrated sensitivity of approximately 86% and specificity of approximately 74% for major depressive disorder in studies using structured diagnostic interviews as the reference standard. These figures vary across populations and reference criteria; some studies using DSM-IV structured interviews report somewhat different operating characteristics. The CES-D is generally considered to have good sensitivity (able to detect most true cases) with somewhat lower specificity compared to instruments developed specifically for clinical diagnosis. Factor analyses of the CES-D have typically identified four factors: depressed affect, positive affect, somatic complaints, and interpersonal difficulties. The positive affect subscale items, which are reverse-scored, contribute to the instrument's ability to distinguish depression from the absence of positive wellbeing — a distinction that has theoretical and clinical significance. Scoring and Interpretation The CES-D uses a four-point response scale. For most items, responses are scored 0 (rarely or none of the time), 1 (some or a little of the time), 2 (occasionally or a moderate amount of time), or 3 (most or all of the time). Four items — numbers 4, 8, 12, and 16 in the original numbering — are phrased positively and are reverse scored: a response of "most or all of the time" on a positive item scores 0, not 3. This reverse-scoring design serves as a check on response acquiescence bias and adds a positive wellbeing dimension. Scores are summed to produce a total between 0 and 60. The most widely used cut-point for identifying probable depression is 16 or above. Scores of 0 to 15 are generally considered to indicate minimal depressive symptoms, while scores of 16 and above suggest significant depressive symptomatology warranting further evaluation. Some researchers have proposed a higher threshold of 23 or 27 for identifying cases more consistent with major depressive disorder specifically. Population and Research Use The CES-D has been used in hundreds of epidemiological studies worldwide, including landmark projects such as the Epidemiologic Catchment Area (ECA) surveys, the Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging, and numerous national health surveys. It has been validated in adolescents, elderly adults, cancer patients, HIV-positive individuals, and diverse cultural and linguistic groups across North America, Europe, Asia, and Africa. The scale's suitability for community surveys stems from its relative brevity combined with its breadth of symptom coverage. It was specifically designed to measure the full spectrum of depressive symptoms rather than only those meeting clinical thresholds, allowing researchers to study depression as a continuously distributed dimension in the general population rather than a binary present-or-absent condition. Strengths The CES-D is one of the most extensively validated depression measures in existence, with decades of research establishing its performance across extremely diverse populations and contexts. Its public domain status has made it a standard tool in global health research. The inclusion of positively worded reverse-scored items provides a more nuanced picture of mood state than scales measuring only negative symptoms, and its 20-item breadth offers greater coverage of the symptom spectrum than shorter instruments. For longitudinal research, the CES-D has an established track record spanning over four decades, allowing direct comparison of findings across studies. Its use in aging research is particularly well established, and age-adjusted norms are available for older adult populations. Limitations The CES-D is longer than most clinical screening tools, requiring approximately five to ten minutes to complete. Its one-week recall window differs from the two-week window of the PHQ-9, which can complicate direct comparison between the instruments. The scale was not designed for clinical diagnosis and cannot substitute for a structured clinical interview. The four reverse-scored items require careful attention during administration, particularly in settings where respondents may not read instructions closely. Misunderstanding of the reversed response format is a known source of scoring error in self-administered surveys. Additionally, the somatic items (sleep, appetite, energy) can be confounded by physical illness, particularly in medically ill older adults, inflating CES-D scores in those populations. Relationship to Other Instruments In clinical settings, the PHQ-9 is generally preferred over the CES-D because of its direct mapping to DSM diagnostic criteria and its more established primary care validation. The CES-D remains the preferred instrument for epidemiological and longitudinal population research, particularly in aging studies. The two instruments measure overlapping constructs but are not interchangeable; studies comparing them in the same samples find moderate to strong correlation but systematic differences at the item level. For wellbeing measurement complementing the CES-D's depression focus, the WHO-5 Well-Being Index provides a positively framed perspective. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician A CES-D score of 16 or above warrants discussion with a healthcare professional for further evaluation. Even scores below 16 that are accompanied by persistent distress, functional difficulty, or safety concerns should prompt a clinical consultation. The CES-D score is a starting point for conversation, not a definitive assessment. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The CES-D is a validated research and screening instrument. A score above the threshold indicates a higher likelihood of clinically significant depressive symptoms and suggests that further professional evaluation is appropriate. It does not constitute a clinical diagnosis of major depressive disorder or any other condition.

---

## /compare

Compare Screeners phq9.online offers seven validated, public-domain screening instruments covering depression, anxiety, functional impairment, and general wellbeing. The table below summarises the key characteristics of each instrument to help you choose the most appropriate tool. Instrument Domain Items Response scale Approx. minutes Licence Primary citation PHQ-9 Depression 9 0–3 (frequency, 4-point); total 0–27 2–3 Public domain (Pfizer waiver) Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB. J Gen Intern Med. 2001;16(9):606–613. doi: 10.1046/j.1525-1497.2001.016009606.x PHQ-2 Depression (ultra-brief screen) 2 0–3 (frequency, 4-point); total 0–6 &lt;1 Public domain (Pfizer waiver) Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB. Med Care. 2003;41(11):1284–1292. doi: 10.1097/01.MLR.0000093487.78664.3C GAD-7 Anxiety (generalised anxiety disorder) 7 0–3 (frequency, 4-point); total 0–21 1–2 Public domain (Pfizer waiver) Spitzer RL, Kroenke K, Williams JB, Löwe B. Arch Intern Med. 2006;166(10):1092–1097. doi: 10.1001/archinte.166.10.1092 CES-D Depression (research / epidemiology) 20 0–3 (frequency, 4-point, 4 items reverse-scored); total 0–60 5–10 Public domain (NIMH) Radloff LS. Appl Psychol Meas. 1977;1(3):385–401. doi: 10.1177/014662167700100306 PROMIS Depression Depression (IRT-calibrated) 8 1–5 (frequency, 5-point); total 8–40; T-score conversion available 2–3 Public domain (NIH PROMIS) Pilkonis PA et al. Assessment. 2011;18(3):263–283. doi: 10.1177/1073191111411667 WSAS Functional impairment 5 0–8 (impairment, 9-point per domain); total 0–40 2–3 Public domain Mundt JC, Marks IM, Shear MK, Greist JH. Br J Psychiatry. 2002;180:461–464. doi: 10.1192/bjp.180.5.461 WHO-5 Wellbeing (positive) 5 0–5 (frequency, 6-point); raw 0–25 multiplied by 4 = 0–100 1 Free to use with attribution to WHO World Health Organization. WHO (Five) Well-Being Index (1998 version). WHO Regional Office for Europe, Copenhagen. who-5.org How to Choose a Screener The right instrument depends on your purpose: Routine depression check: The PHQ-9 is the most widely validated and broadly used option. It is suitable for most adults in primary care and community settings. Quick initial screen: The PHQ-2 can be completed in under a minute. A positive result (score 3 or above) should prompt administration of the full PHQ-9. Anxiety screening: The GAD-7 is the standard choice for generalised anxiety disorder. It is also used dimensionally across anxiety disorders more broadly. Research or epidemiology: The CES-D is widely used in population studies and is particularly sensitive to subclinical depressive symptoms. IRT-based or NIH research context: The PROMIS Depression Short Form provides T-score-based measurement suitable for comparing across populations. Functional impairment assessment: Use the WSAS alongside any symptom screener to assess how much symptoms are affecting work, home, and social life. General wellbeing monitoring: The WHO-5 measures positive wellbeing rather than symptom severity and is useful for longitudinal tracking of overall mood state. All seven instruments are free to use and are in the public domain or have equivalent open licensing. See the scoring guide for full details on how each instrument is scored and what the bands mean. Notes on the Table Approx. minutes refers to estimated self-completion time under typical conditions. Actual time will vary by individual and administration context. PROMIS T-scores: The PROMIS Depression Short Form 8a produces a raw score (8–40) which can be converted to a T-score (population mean of 50, SD of 10) using conversion tables provided by the NIH HealthMeasures programme. The raw score bands shown in the scoring guide are approximations. For precise T-score conversion in research contexts, use the HealthMeasures Scoring Service . CES-D reverse scoring: Four items on the CES-D (covering positive affect) are reverse-scored: a response of 3 contributes 0 to the total, and a response of 0 contributes 3. This is handled automatically on this site. WHO-5 scaling: The WHO-5 raw score (0–25) is multiplied by 4 to produce a percentage score on a 0–100 scale. This conversion is handled automatically on this site. Higher scores indicate better wellbeing; lower scores indicate poorer wellbeing and possible depression requiring further evaluation.

---

## /contact

Contact For questions, feedback, content corrections, or general enquiries, please email contact@phq9.online . The maintainer aims to respond within a few business days, though response times may vary depending on volume. For bug reports, feature requests, or technical issues, the preferred channel is GitHub Issues . Opening an issue there allows others to see the discussion and contribute, and makes it easier to track progress on any reported problems. Please note that this site does not provide clinical or medical advice. If you have a mental health concern, please contact a qualified healthcare professional or, if you are in crisis, a crisis helpline. See the crisis resources page for contact numbers in your country. If you are contacting us about a screener error, a broken link, or a factual inaccuracy in the content, please include the page URL and a brief description of the issue. We take accuracy seriously and aim to address substantive content corrections promptly. The maintainer is based in Iceland. Response times are typically within a few business days, but may be longer during busy periods. For time-sensitive technical matters, GitHub Issues tends to be monitored more frequently than the contact email address and is the preferred channel for anything related to screener accuracy or site bugs.

---

## /crisis

Crisis Resources If you are in immediate danger, call your local emergency services now: 911 (US, Canada), 999 (UK), 112 (EU, including Iceland), 000 (Australia), or 110 / 112 (Germany). If you are not in immediate danger but are having thoughts of suicide or self-harm, please reach out to a crisis service below. All phone lines listed are free unless noted otherwise. Many also offer text, web chat, and email options for those who prefer not to speak aloud. United States 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline — Call or text 988 (24/7, free). Also available via chat at 988lifeline.org . Specialised options for veterans (press 1), LGBTQ+ individuals, and Spanish speakers. Crisis Text Line — Text HOME to 741741 (24/7, free). Text-based crisis support with trained counsellors. United Kingdom Samaritans — Call 116 123 (24/7, free from any phone in the UK and Ireland). Also available at samaritans.org via email (jo@samaritans.org — response within 24 hours). Shout — Text SHOUT to 85258 (24/7, free). Text-based crisis support via giveusashout.org . PAPYRUS (under 35s) — Call 0800 068 4141 (Mon–Fri 10am–10pm, weekends 2pm–10pm). Text 07786 209697. Canada 988 Suicide Crisis Helpline — Call or text 988 (24/7, free, available in English and French). Launched November 2023. Crisis Services Canada — Call 1-833-456-4566 (24/7). Text 45645 (4pm–midnight ET). Iceland Pieta / Throskahjálp — Call 1717 (24/7, free). The national suicide prevention helpline operated by Pieta in cooperation with Icelandic health authorities. Australia Lifeline — Call 13 11 14 (24/7, free). Also available via web chat at lifeline.org.au . Beyond Blue — Call 1300 22 4636 (24/7). Also available via web chat at beyondblue.org.au . Germany TelefonSeelsorge — Call 0800 111 0 111 or 0800 111 0 222 (24/7, free). Confidential emotional support and crisis counselling. Also available via web chat and email at telefonseelsorge.de . France Numéro national de prévention du suicide — Call 3114 (24/7, free). The French national suicide prevention hotline staffed by mental health professionals. Also available via chat at 3114.fr . International Directory If your country is not listed above, the following resources provide comprehensive international directories: findahelpline.com — searchable directory of crisis lines covering over 100 countries, including phone, text, and chat services. International Association for Suicide Prevention — Crisis Centres — directory maintained by the IASP. Text and Chat Services For those who prefer not to use the phone, text and web chat options are available in many countries. Key services include: Crisis Text Line (US/UK/Canada/Ireland) — Free 24/7 text-based support. Text HOME to 741741 (US/Canada), 85258 (UK), or 50808 (Ireland). 988 Chat (US) — Available at 988lifeline.org/chat . 3114 Chat (France) — Available at 3114.fr . What to Expect When You Call Many people hesitate to call a crisis line because they are unsure what to expect. Crisis lines are staffed by trained volunteers or professionals who will listen without judgment. You do not need to be at the point of attempting suicide to call — you can reach out whenever you are in distress, overwhelmed, or struggling to cope. Calls are confidential. The counsellor will listen, help you think through your situation, and may provide information about local resources. You are in control of the conversation and can end it at any time. If you are unable to speak or prefer not to use the phone, most of the services listed above also offer text or web chat options. Some services also support email contact if you need more time to express yourself in writing. You deserve support. Reaching out is a sign of strength, not weakness. If one service does not feel right for you, try another — they all exist to help.

---

## /disclaimer

Disclaimer Not Medical Advice The content on phq9.online, including all screening questionnaires, scoring results, interpretive text, and informational articles, is provided for general informational and educational purposes only. Nothing on this site constitutes medical advice, clinical guidance, diagnosis, or treatment. You should not use the results of any screener on this site as the basis for starting, stopping, or modifying any medical treatment, including medication, therapy, or other interventions. Screening Is Not Diagnosis The instruments available on this site — including the PHQ-9, PHQ-2, GAD-7, CES-D, PROMIS Depression, WSAS, and WHO-5 — are validated screening tools, not diagnostic tests. Screening is a population-level process of identifying individuals who may benefit from further clinical evaluation. A score above a cut-point indicates that your responses resemble those of people who were subsequently found to meet diagnostic criteria in a clinical assessment, but it does not constitute a diagnosis of any mental health condition. A low score does not guarantee that you do not have depression, anxiety, or any other condition. Mental health symptoms exist on a spectrum, and significant distress can be present at any score level. If you are concerned about your mental health, speak with a qualified healthcare professional regardless of your screening result. No Doctor-Patient Relationship Using this website does not create a doctor-patient relationship, a therapist-client relationship, or any other professional relationship between you and the maintainer of phq9.online or any third party associated with this site. The maintainer is not a licensed healthcare provider and does not provide clinical services of any kind. Seek Professional Advice Always consult a qualified and licensed healthcare provider — such as a general practitioner, psychiatrist, psychologist, or other mental health professional — before making any decisions about your mental health or treatment. They are the appropriate people to evaluate your specific circumstances, order relevant assessments, and provide personalised advice. Crisis and Emergency Situations If you are experiencing a mental health emergency, are in immediate danger, or are having thoughts of suicide or self-harm, do not rely on this website for help. Contact your local emergency services immediately (911 in the US, 999 in the UK, 112 in the EU, 000 in Australia), or call a dedicated crisis helpline. A list of crisis services is available on the crisis resources page. Accuracy and Currency While every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy of the screening instruments and supporting information on this site, the maintainer makes no representations or warranties of any kind — express or implied — as to the completeness, accuracy, reliability, or suitability of the information for any purpose. The field of mental health research evolves continuously; clinical guidelines and scoring thresholds may change. Users are encouraged to consult current clinical guidelines and primary literature.

---

## /faq

Frequently Asked Questions What is the PHQ-9? The PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) is a validated nine-item self-report screening instrument used to detect and measure the severity of depressive symptoms. It was developed by Drs. Robert Spitzer, Janet Williams, and Kurt Kroenke as part of the PRIME-MD project and first validated in a study of 6,000 primary care patients published in 2001. The instrument is in the public domain and is used worldwide in clinical, research, and population health settings. Is the PHQ-9 free to use? Yes. The PHQ-9 is in the public domain. It was originally developed with support from an educational grant from Pfizer Inc., but Pfizer waived all rights to the instrument. It can be reproduced, translated, and used without permission or payment. The other instruments on this site — the PHQ-2, GAD-7, CES-D, PROMIS Depression, WSAS, and WHO-5 — are also in the public domain. Is this a diagnosis? No. A score from any instrument on this site is not a diagnosis. These are screening tools designed to identify people who may benefit from further clinical evaluation. A high score indicates that your pattern of responses resembles that of people who were subsequently found to have the condition by a structured clinical interview, but only a qualified clinician who has conducted a full assessment can provide a diagnosis. How accurate is the PHQ-9? At the standard cut-point of 10 or greater, the PHQ-9 has demonstrated sensitivity of approximately 88% and specificity of approximately 88% for major depressive disorder in primary care settings, based on the original 2001 validation study of 6,000 patients. A 2010 meta-analysis across 38 independent studies confirmed these estimates, reporting pooled sensitivity of 88% (95% CI 83–92%) and specificity of 85% (95% CI 82–88%). What does my score mean? PHQ-9 scores fall into five bands: 0–4 (minimal or no depression), 5–9 (mild), 10–14 (moderate), 15–19 (moderately severe), and 20–27 (severe). Scores should be interpreted alongside your clinical context. A score at any level does not automatically require a specific action, but a score of 10 or above generally warrants a conversation with a healthcare professional. See the full interpretation guide . What is the GAD-7? The GAD-7 (Generalised Anxiety Disorder Scale-7) is a seven-item self-report instrument that assesses symptoms of generalised anxiety disorder over the past two weeks. It uses the same four-point frequency scale as the PHQ-9. Scores range from 0 to 21, with bands at 0–4 (minimal), 5–9 (mild), 10–14 (moderate), and 15–21 (severe). A cut-point of 10 was validated against structured clinical interview in a study published in 2006 (doi:10.1001/archinte.166.10.1092). What is the CES-D? The CES-D (Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale) is a 20-item self-report depression scale developed at the National Institute of Mental Health by Lenore Radloff in 1977. It was designed for use in epidemiological research and covers depressed mood, somatic symptoms, positive affect (reverse-scored), and interpersonal problems over the past week. Scores range from 0 to 60. A score of 16 or above is the standard screening threshold. What is the WHO-5? The WHO-5 Well-Being Index is a five-item questionnaire developed by the World Health Organization that assesses subjective psychological wellbeing over the past two weeks using positive statements. The raw score (0–25) is multiplied by four to produce a percentage score from 0 to 100. Higher scores indicate better wellbeing. A score below 50 is used as a threshold for further depression assessment, and a score below 28 suggests likely depression. What is the WSAS? The Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) is a five-item instrument that measures functional impairment across five life domains: work, home management, social leisure, private leisure, and close relationships. Each item is rated 0 (not impaired) to 8 (very severely impaired), giving a total range of 0 to 40. It is used alongside symptom-focused screeners to assess whether symptoms are causing meaningful disruption to daily life. What is the PROMIS Depression Short Form? The PROMIS Depression Short Form 8a is an eight-item self-report instrument developed by the National Institutes of Health as part of the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System. Items are rated on a five-point scale from "never" to "always" over the past seven days. It is calibrated using item response theory, which allows comparison across populations. The raw score ranges from 8 to 40. PROMIS instruments are in the public domain. Does the site store my answers or personal data? No answers or personal data are sent to any server. If you opt in to saving your history, screener attempts are stored only in your browser's localStorage on your own device. No account, registration, or login is required. The site does not use analytics cookies by default. Google AdSense may set cookies in relation to advertising if ads are shown; see the privacy policy for full details on how data is handled. LocalStorage data can be exported or deleted at any time from the history page. What should I do if I scored high? A high score is a prompt to seek further evaluation, not a cause for alarm. If you scored 10 or above on the PHQ-9, or in the moderate to severe range on any other instrument, consider speaking with your general practitioner or a mental health professional. They can conduct a thorough assessment, rule out other medical or situational causes, and discuss appropriate support or evidence-based treatment options including psychological therapy and, where indicated, medication. You do not need to wait until symptoms become severe before seeking help. Earlier evaluation generally leads to better outcomes. What should I do if I am having thoughts of suicide or self-harm? Please contact a crisis service immediately. In the US, call or text 988. In the UK, call Samaritans on 116 123 (free, available 24 hours). In Canada, call or text 988. In Australia, call Lifeline on 13 11 14. In Germany, call TelefonSeelsorge on 0800 111 0 111. In France, call 3114. In Iceland, call Pieta on 1717. For a full list of international resources, visit the crisis resources page or go to findahelpline.com . If you are in immediate danger, call local emergency services (911, 999, 112, or 110 depending on your country). Can I use the PHQ-9 to monitor my treatment progress? Yes. One of the most valuable uses of the PHQ-9 is longitudinal monitoring. Repeated assessments at regular intervals — for example, monthly or at each clinical appointment — allow both individuals and clinicians to track whether symptoms are improving, stable, or worsening over the course of treatment. A reduction of 5 or more points from a baseline score is generally considered a clinically meaningful improvement. Response is typically defined as a 50% reduction in score, and remission as a score below 5. The history page on this site allows you to save and review past attempts if you opt in to local history storage. Are these instruments validated in languages other than English? Yes. The PHQ-9 has been translated into more than 80 languages and validated in numerous cultural contexts including Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, Hindi, and Portuguese. The GAD-7, WHO-5, and other instruments on this site are also widely translated and validated internationally. While psychometric properties are generally replicated, minor differences in performance across cultures have been identified, particularly for somatic symptom items such as sleep and appetite. The instruments on this site are currently presented in English; localised versions may be added in future updates. Who should not rely on these screeners? These screeners are not validated for young children, and specialised instruments are available for adolescents and older adults that may be more appropriate in those age groups. The Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) and the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) are examples of instruments adapted for specific populations. People with significant cognitive impairment or acute psychosis may not be able to complete self-report instruments accurately. The PHQ-9 should not be the sole screening tool when bipolar disorder is a concern, as it does not assess manic or hypomanic symptoms. Anyone using a screener in a clinical context should consult current professional guidelines for their specific population. How is phq9.online funded? The site is funded through Google AdSense advertising. Ads may be shown to visitors in accordance with Google's advertising policies. The maintainer may also accept direct sponsorships from organisations whose products or services are relevant to the site's audience. Instrument selection and content are independent of funding sources; only public-domain, evidence-based instruments are included. The site's source code is open and available for review. What is the difference between screening and diagnosis? Screening is a population-level process of identifying individuals who are likely to have a condition and who should receive further clinical evaluation. A screening test is designed to be sensitive — that is, to miss as few true cases as possible — at the cost of some false positives. Diagnosis, in contrast, is a clinical judgment made by a qualified professional after a thorough assessment that takes into account symptom history, duration, functional impact, differential diagnosis, and the patient's full medical context. The PHQ-9 and other instruments on this site are screening tools. They can support clinical decision-making but cannot replace it. Is phq9.online affiliated with Pfizer, Spitzer, Kroenke, or any health authority? No. phq9.online is an independent website maintained by a private individual (nathanib). It is not affiliated with Pfizer, the instrument authors, the NHS, the WHO, or any government health authority. The instruments are reproduced from public-domain sources. The site does not provide clinical services and has no commercial relationship with any healthcare provider. For the full source code, see github.com/Evoke4350/phq9-online .

---

## /gad-7

What Is the GAD-7? The Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7) is a seven-item self-administered questionnaire designed to screen for and measure the severity of generalized anxiety disorder in clinical and community settings. It asks respondents to rate how often, over the previous two weeks, they have been bothered by seven core anxiety symptoms drawn from the DSM diagnostic criteria for generalised anxiety disorder. These symptoms include feeling nervous or on edge, not being able to stop or control worrying, worrying too much about different things, trouble relaxing, being so restless it is hard to sit still, becoming easily annoyed or irritable, and feeling afraid as if something awful might happen. The GAD-7 has become one of the most widely used anxiety measures internationally. It is the standard anxiety screening instrument in NHS Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) services in England, is included in CANMAT clinical guidelines in Canada, and is routinely used in research studies, large epidemiological surveys, and primary care quality improvement programmes. History and Development The GAD-7 was developed by Robert L. Spitzer, Kurt Kroenke, Janet B. W. Williams, and Bernd Löwe, and was first described in a paper published in Archives of Internal Medicine in 2006 (doi:10.1001/archinte.166.10.1092). The development followed the same approach as the PHQ-9: items were drawn from the DSM criteria for generalised anxiety disorder, the scale was self-administered, and validation was conducted in large primary care samples. The instrument was developed with support from Pfizer Inc. as part of the broader Patient Health Questionnaire programme and is available in the public domain without restriction. The timing of the GAD-7 publication came at a point when clinicians had growing awareness of anxiety disorders as a major contributor to primary care consultations but lacked a brief, validated measure that could complement the PHQ-9 in routine screening. The two instruments were designed to work together, and their shared response format and scoring approach make them practical to administer as a pair. Psychometric Properties The 2006 validation study enrolled 2,740 patients across 15 primary care clinics and compared GAD-7 scores to structured clinical interviews. Using a cut-point of 10 or greater for generalised anxiety disorder, the GAD-7 demonstrated a sensitivity of 89% and a specificity of 82%. Subsequent studies have reported slightly varying figures depending on population and reference standard, with sensitivity typically ranging from 80% to 92% and specificity from 76% to 88% across different settings and cut-points. Internal consistency is high, with Cronbach's alpha consistently above 0.89 in validation studies. Test-retest reliability in stable patients has been reported at 0.83. The GAD-7 also shows good convergent validity with established anxiety measures including the Beck Anxiety Inventory, the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale anxiety subscale, and the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory. An important feature of the GAD-7 is that while it was designed for generalised anxiety disorder, research has shown it also screens reasonably well for other anxiety disorders including panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, and post-traumatic stress disorder. This broad sensitivity to anxiety psychopathology, rather than specificity for GAD alone, makes it useful as a general anxiety indicator in primary care settings. Scoring and Severity Bands Each of the seven items is rated 0 (not at all), 1 (several days), 2 (more than half the days), or 3 (nearly every day). The total score ranges from 0 to 21. The four severity bands established in the original validation are as follows: 0–4: Minimal anxiety. Symptoms at this level are unlikely to represent a clinical anxiety disorder. Monitoring is appropriate if symptoms are new or worsening. 5–9: Mild anxiety. Watchful waiting is appropriate. Consider lifestyle factors and psychoeducation. Reassess in four to six weeks. 10–14: Moderate anxiety. Further evaluation and consideration of treatment with psychological therapy or pharmacotherapy is recommended. 15–21: Severe anxiety. Active treatment is indicated. Referral to a mental health specialist should be strongly considered. Anxiety and Depression Comorbidity Anxiety and depression are the two most commonly co-occurring mental health conditions. Population-based studies consistently find that 40% to 60% of individuals with major depressive disorder also meet criteria for an anxiety disorder, and vice versa. This high comorbidity rate is one of the primary reasons the GAD-7 is routinely administered alongside the PHQ-9: a person presenting with a PHQ-9 score of 10 or above may have a concurrent anxiety disorder that shapes the clinical presentation, affects prognosis, and has implications for treatment selection. When both conditions are present, treatment complexity increases. Cognitive behavioural therapy protocols need to address both presentations, and some antidepressant medications are more effective than others when anxiety is prominent. Having quantitative scores on both dimensions at baseline also enables tracking of both symptom clusters over time as treatment progresses. NHS IAPT Use In England, the NHS IAPT programme mandates the PHQ-9 and GAD-7 as the minimum data set for all adult patients entering psychological therapy services. Both measures are administered at every session, allowing clinicians and service managers to monitor symptom trajectories across the full episode of care. This has generated one of the largest real-world datasets of anxiety and depression outcomes in existence, providing evidence that brief psychological therapies are effective at population scale. Recovery is defined in IAPT as moving from above to below the clinical threshold on both measures simultaneously. Strengths The GAD-7 is brief, free to use, and well validated across a wide range of populations and settings. Its scoring is transparent and easily explained to patients. The shared format with the PHQ-9 minimises the cognitive switch required when answering both questionnaires in sequence, which reduces respondent burden. The instrument is available in numerous validated translations and has been used successfully in non-English-speaking populations across Europe, Asia, Latin America, and Africa. Limitations The GAD-7 was developed and validated specifically for generalised anxiety disorder and cannot diagnose or distinguish between specific anxiety disorders such as social anxiety disorder, panic disorder, agoraphobia, or specific phobias. A high score indicates probable clinically significant anxiety but does not specify the subtype or aetiology. Some items, particularly those about irritability and restlessness, can be elevated in conditions other than anxiety, including hyperthyroidism, stimulant use, mania, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, which may produce false positives in some populations. The instrument also relies on self-report over a two-week recall window, which introduces recall bias. Patients in acute distress may report more symptoms than would be captured over a structured clinical interview, while patients minimising their difficulties may underreport. Relationship to Other Instruments The GAD-7 is most commonly paired with the PHQ-9 in clinical practice and research. For functional impairment, the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) is a validated complement that quantifies how anxiety or depression affects everyday activities. For patients in whom panic disorder is specifically suspected, the PHQ-Panic module provides additional screening specificity. In research contexts, the GAD-7 is frequently used alongside longer measures such as the Penn State Worry Questionnaire for discriminant validity studies. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician A GAD-7 score of 10 or above warrants a conversation with a general practitioner or mental health professional. Lower scores that are still causing distress or functional difficulty also warrant discussion. Anxiety disorders are among the most treatable of mental health conditions, and effective psychological therapies — particularly cognitive behavioural therapy — are available through NHS IAPT and similar services internationally without requiring a psychiatrist referral in most healthcare systems. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The GAD-7 is a validated self-report screening instrument designed to support clinical decision-making. A score above the threshold suggests that further evaluation is warranted; it cannot, on its own, establish a clinical diagnosis of generalised anxiety disorder or any other condition. Diagnosis requires a comprehensive assessment by a qualified clinician.

---

## /history

History {m.history_saved()} {m.history_optin_label()} download('phq9-history.json', 'application/json', exportJson())} class="rounded bg-[color:var(--color-accent)] px-3 py-1 text-white">{m.history_export_json()} download('phq9-history.csv', 'text/csv', exportCsv())} class="rounded bg-[color:var(--color-accent)] px-3 py-1 text-white">{m.history_export_csv()} { clearAll(); attempts = []; }} class="rounded border px-3 py-1">{m.history_clear_all()} Date Screener Score Band Actions {#each attempts as a} {new Date(a.completedAt).toLocaleString()} {a.screenerId} {a.score} {a.band} { deleteAttempt(a.id); attempts = listAttempts(); }} aria-label="Delete" class="underline">Delete {/each}

---

## /interpretation

Interpreting Your Score A screener score is a number on a continuum, not a verdict. Understanding what the number means — and what it does not mean — is the first step toward using it constructively. This page explains what different severity bands typically indicate across the instruments available on phq9.online, when professional support is advisable, and a general overview of how depression and anxiety are treated when they are identified. None of the information here constitutes medical advice, diagnosis, or a recommendation to start or stop any treatment. These instruments are screening tools. Only a qualified clinician who has assessed you fully can provide a diagnosis and recommend a treatment plan. What a Screening Score Tells You Screening instruments are designed to be sensitive: they aim to identify as many people who may have the condition as possible, accepting a degree of false positives. A score above a cut-point means that your pattern of responses resembles that of people in the validation studies who were subsequently confirmed to have the condition by a clinical interview. It does not mean you have been diagnosed. Conversely, a score below the cut-point does not guarantee you are well. Depression exists on a spectrum, and people with subclinical scores can still experience significant distress. If you are worried about how you feel regardless of your score, speaking to a doctor or counsellor is always appropriate. Minimal / No Symptoms Scores in the minimal or no-symptom range — PHQ-9 0–4, GAD-7 0–4, CES-D 0–15, WSAS 0, WHO-5 51–100 — suggest that you are not currently reporting symptoms at a level associated with clinical disorder. This is a broadly positive finding, but it should be understood in context: Some people minimise symptoms when completing questionnaires, particularly in a first assessment or when there is perceived stigma around mental health. Symptoms fluctuate. A minimal score today does not mean symptoms cannot worsen in coming weeks. If you have had prior episodes of depression or anxiety, periodic rescreening can detect early changes. Wellbeing is broader than the absence of symptoms. A minimal depression score does not capture burnout, chronic stress, relationship difficulties, or other sources of distress that might warrant attention. Mild Symptoms Scores in the mild range — PHQ-9 5–9, GAD-7 5–9, CES-D 16 (at threshold), WSAS 1–9, WHO-5 29–50 — suggest the presence of some symptoms that are meaningful but not yet at levels typically associated with a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate intervention. At this level, clinicians commonly recommend: Watchful waiting: Rescreening in four to six weeks to see whether symptoms are improving, stable, or worsening. Lifestyle review: Sleep regularity, physical activity (30 minutes of moderate aerobic activity most days has robust evidence for mood benefit), reducing alcohol consumption, maintaining social contact, and managing workload where possible. Psychoeducation: Learning about the nature of depression and anxiety, including the evidence that they are treatable conditions and that most episodes improve with or without formal intervention. Low-intensity psychological support: Some health systems offer guided self-help, computerised cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT), or group-based support for mild symptoms without a formal referral. Mild symptoms should not be dismissed. For many people, mild presentations escalate without support, whereas early intervention at this stage can prevent progression to more severe episodes. Moderate Symptoms Scores in the moderate range — PHQ-9 10–14, GAD-7 10–14, WSAS 10–19, WHO-5 29–50 with additional indicators — are associated with meaningful interference in daily functioning and generally warrant a clinical assessment. At this level you should consider speaking to your general practitioner, a psychiatrist, or a licensed mental health professional. Moderate-severity presentations are the level at which evidence-based psychological and pharmacological treatments are typically recommended. Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) has the strongest evidence base across both depression and anxiety disorders. Interpersonal therapy (IPT), behavioural activation, and mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) also have well-replicated evidence. Antidepressant medication may be appropriate depending on the clinical picture, comorbidities, and patient preferences; this is a decision to make with a prescribing clinician, not on the basis of a screening score. Moderately Severe and Severe Symptoms Scores in the moderately severe and severe ranges — PHQ-9 15–19 (moderately severe) and 20–27 (severe), GAD-7 15–21 (severe), WSAS 20–40 (severe), WHO-5 0–28 (very low) — are associated with substantial distress and significant impairment in work, social, and personal functioning. Seeking professional support as soon as possible is strongly advised. At severe levels, assessment by a clinician is important to: Confirm or rule out a formal diagnosis and rule out other medical causes of symptoms. Assess safety, including the presence of thoughts of self-harm or suicide. Develop a structured treatment plan that may include psychological therapy, medication, crisis planning, and coordination with other health services. You do not need to be in immediate danger to seek help. Feeling severely depressed or anxious is itself sufficient reason to contact a healthcare provider. When to Seek Help Immediately Contact emergency services or go to your nearest emergency department immediately if you: Are having thoughts of suicide or self-harm and have a plan or intention to act. Have made an attempt to harm yourself. Feel you may be a danger to yourself or others. If you are not in immediate danger but are having recurring thoughts of suicide or self-harm, contact a crisis service now. See the crisis resources page for helpline numbers in your country, including 988 (US), 116 123 Samaritans (UK), 988 (Canada), 13 11 14 Lifeline (Australia), and others. PHQ-9 item 9 asks directly about thoughts of self-harm or being better off dead. Any response other than "not at all" to this item — regardless of the total score — warrants careful clinical attention. Overview of Treatment Approaches This section provides a general overview of recognised treatment approaches for depression and anxiety. It is not a treatment recommendation and is not a substitute for advice from a qualified clinician who knows your individual circumstances. Psychological Therapies Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) is the most extensively studied psychological treatment for depression and generalised anxiety disorder. CBT addresses unhelpful thinking patterns and behaviours that maintain low mood and worry. It is typically delivered over 8 to 20 sessions, either individually or in a group format, and can also be delivered via digital platforms. Other evidence-supported psychological approaches include: Interpersonal therapy (IPT): Focuses on grief, role transitions, and interpersonal conflicts as triggers for depression. Behavioural activation (BA): A component of CBT that targets the withdrawal and avoidance behaviours that perpetuate depression by increasing engagement with rewarding activities. Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT): Particularly effective for preventing relapse in people who have had three or more previous depressive episodes. Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT): Focuses on psychological flexibility and values-based action rather than symptom elimination. Pharmacological Treatment Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are the first-line pharmacological treatment for moderate to severe depression and for most anxiety disorders. Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) are an alternative with similar efficacy. Medication decisions must be made with a prescribing clinician, who will consider your individual medical history, current medications, and preferences. Medication and psychological therapy in combination produce better outcomes than either alone in moderate to severe depression, according to multiple clinical trials. Lifestyle and Self-Management Regular aerobic exercise is one of the best-evidenced lifestyle interventions for mild to moderate depression, with effect sizes comparable to antidepressant medication in several randomised trials. Consistent sleep patterns, limiting alcohol and cannabis use, and maintaining social contact are also associated with better mood regulation. These approaches complement formal treatment but are not a substitute for it when symptoms are moderate to severe. Understanding Your Score in Relation to WSAS The WSAS measures functional impairment rather than symptom severity. A high WSAS score alongside moderate or severe symptoms on the PHQ-9 or GAD-7 provides additional evidence that the condition is having a significant impact on daily life and warrants prompt clinical attention. A low WSAS score alongside elevated symptom scores may indicate that the individual is coping functionally despite distress, which does not reduce the importance of seeking support but may affect urgency and the choice of intervention. Longitudinal Monitoring One of the most valuable uses of validated screeners is tracking change over time. Repeated assessments at regular intervals — for example, monthly or at each clinical appointment — allow both the individual and their clinician to see whether symptoms are improving, stable, or worsening. A decrease of 5 or more points on the PHQ-9 from baseline is generally considered a clinically meaningful improvement. If you are in treatment and your score is not improving after several weeks, discuss this with your clinician. Use the history page to review your past screening attempts if you have opted in to saving them on this device.

---

## /phq-2

What Is the PHQ-2? The Patient Health Questionnaire-2 (PHQ-2) is a two-item ultra-brief screening instrument designed to detect probable depression with minimal patient burden. It asks about the two cardinal symptoms of major depressive disorder — depressed mood and anhedonia (loss of interest or pleasure) — over the preceding two weeks. Each item is rated on a four-point frequency scale identical to the full PHQ-9, giving a total score range of 0 to 6. The PHQ-2 functions as a first-stage triage tool: a positive result (typically a score of 3 or higher) prompts the clinician or patient to proceed with the full nine-item PHQ-9 to assess all symptom dimensions of depression and establish severity. Despite its brevity, the PHQ-2 captures the two symptoms that define a depressive episode according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. Most clinicians and researchers consider that at least one of these two items must be endorsed for a major depressive episode to be diagnosed, which means the PHQ-2 has good face validity as a gateway screen. History and Development The PHQ-2 was developed by Kurt Kroenke, Robert L. Spitzer, and Janet B. W. Williams, the same team responsible for the broader Patient Health Questionnaire family of screeners. It was validated and described in a paper published in the journal Medical Care in 2003 (doi:10.1097/01.MLR.0000093487.78664.3C). The two items were not newly created for the PHQ-2; they are simply the first two questions of the PHQ-9, which themselves correspond to the two core criteria for major depressive disorder. The innovation was demonstrating that these two items alone, scored together, offered clinically useful screening performance comparable to longer instruments when used as an initial triage gate. The PHQ-2 was developed with support from an educational grant by Pfizer Inc. and is in the public domain, available for unrestricted use without permission or payment. Psychometric Properties The 2003 validation study by Kroenke et al. enrolled 6,000 primary care patients and compared PHQ-2 scores against structured diagnostic interviews. Using a cut-point of 3 or greater, the PHQ-2 achieved a sensitivity of 83% and a specificity of 90% for major depressive disorder. Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve was 0.91, indicating excellent overall discrimination between those with and without depression. These properties have been replicated in independent samples including elderly adults, patients with chronic illness, perinatal populations, and adolescents. One consistent finding is that the PHQ-2 has slightly lower sensitivity than the PHQ-9 when both are used as standalone tools, which is expected given that it measures fewer symptom dimensions. This is why it is recommended as a first-stage screen rather than a complete assessment. The two-step approach — PHQ-2 followed by PHQ-9 when positive — preserves both sensitivity and efficiency in high-throughput settings such as busy general practice surgeries.
