Scoring and Interpretation Each item is scored 0 (not at all), 1 (several days), 2 (more than half the days), or 3 (nearly every day). Scores are summed to produce a total between 0 and 6. The recommended threshold for a positive screen is a total score of 3 or greater. At this threshold, the PHQ-2 performs comparably to many longer screens. A score of 0–2 suggests that major depression is unlikely, though it does not exclude dysthymia, adjustment disorder, or other mood presentations that manifest more subtly. A score of 3–6 indicates a positive screen and should be followed up with the complete PHQ-9 to determine symptom count, severity, and functional impact. Some guidelines also recommend following a positive PHQ-2 with open clinical enquiry about duration, context, and prior history before jumping to formal scoring. Population and Validation Contexts The PHQ-2 has been validated across a wide range of clinical and community populations. Studies have confirmed its utility in adult primary care, geriatric medicine, oncology, obstetrics (screening for perinatal depression), and adolescent health settings. It has been translated into dozens of languages and validated cross-culturally, broadly mirroring the reach of the PHQ-9. The brevity of the instrument makes it especially attractive in settings where patient literacy or cognitive capacity limits tolerance for longer questionnaires. Strengths The PHQ-2 can be administered verbally in under thirty seconds, making it practical for systematic screening during routine clinical encounters such as annual health checks or urgent care visits. It adds negligible burden to workflows where the goal is population-level case finding rather than detailed severity measurement. Its tight relationship to the PHQ-9 means that a positive result naturally leads to a validated instrument that provides all the information needed for treatment planning without the patient needing to start over. Limitations The PHQ-2 cannot determine depression severity, identify specific symptom profiles, or provide the granular information needed to guide treatment decisions. A negative PHQ-2 does not rule out subsyndromal depression or other mental health conditions, and clinicians should not rely solely on a score of 0–2 to dismiss concerns raised by a patient. The instrument also cannot assess suicidality; item 9 of the PHQ-9, which asks about thoughts of self-harm, is absent from the PHQ-2. In any situation where safety is a concern, a more comprehensive assessment is essential regardless of PHQ-2 score. Like the PHQ-9, the PHQ-2 measures frequency of symptoms without addressing their cause. Somatic conditions such as hypothyroidism, anaemia, or sleep apnoea can produce depressive symptoms that inflate scores. A positive screen always warrants clinical evaluation to identify potentially treatable medical causes before attributing symptoms to primary depressive disorder. Relationship to Other Instruments The PHQ-2 is the natural entry point to the PHQ-9 within the Patient Health Questionnaire family. In practice, many clinical protocols use the PHQ-2 as a universal screen administered to all presenting patients and then deploy the PHQ-9 only to those who score positively, conserving clinical time. When anxiety comorbidity is also a concern, the PHQ-9 is frequently paired with the GAD-7; this combination is a mandated minimum dataset in NHS IAPT services in England. For an assessment of functional impairment, the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) provides a validated complement. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician Any PHQ-2 score of 3 or above warrants follow-up with a healthcare professional. Even a score of 1 or 2 should prompt a conversation if you have been feeling persistently low, if symptoms have been worsening, or if you are concerned about your mental health. Early discussion can identify whether a fuller assessment is warranted and whether any immediate support would be helpful. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The PHQ-2 is a validated two-item screening instrument. A positive result indicates that further evaluation is appropriate; it does not constitute a clinical diagnosis of any mental health condition. Only a qualified clinician can diagnose depression after a comprehensive assessment that takes into account your full medical and psychological history.

---

## /privacy

Privacy Policy This privacy policy explains what data phq9.online collects (very little), how it is used, and what rights you have. It applies to all visitors to phq9.online. Last updated: January 2025. No Accounts, No Personally Identifiable Information phq9.online does not require any account, registration, or login. You can use every screener on the site without providing any name, email address, date of birth, or any other personally identifiable information (PII). The site does not collect or store PII on any server operated by the maintainer. Screener Answers and Local History When you complete a screener, your answers are processed entirely in your browser to calculate your score. The answers and your score are not transmitted to any server. If you opt in to saving your history, your completed screener attempts (screener name, date, score, band, and individual answers) are stored in your browser's localStorage . This data stays on your device and is not accessible to the site's server or to any third party. History storage is opt-in and disabled by default. You can enable or disable it at any time from the history page , and you can export or delete all stored data from that page at any time. Clearing your browser's site data will also remove all stored history. Advertising — Google AdSense phq9.online uses Google AdSense to display advertisements. Google AdSense may use cookies and similar tracking technologies to show personalised ads based on your browsing activity, as described in Google's advertising policies . Google's use of advertising cookies enables it and its partners to serve ads based on your visits to this and other websites. You can opt out of personalised advertising by visiting Google Ads Settings or by visiting aboutads.info . Consent Management — Google Funding Choices For visitors in the European Economic Area (EEA), the United Kingdom, and Canada, consent for advertising cookies is collected via the Google Funding Choices Consent Management Platform (CMP). This is a Google-provided tool that meets the requirements of the IAB Europe Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF). You will be presented with a consent notice when you first visit the site if you are in one of these regions. You may change your consent preferences at any time. If you do not consent to advertising cookies, Google will serve non-personalised ads instead. The site remains fully functional regardless of your advertising consent choice. Analytics phq9.online does not use any analytics service by default. No Google Analytics, no Matomo, no Plausible — nothing that tracks page views or user behaviour out of the box. If Cloudflare Web Analytics is added in the future, it is cookieless and privacy-preserving — it does not use cookies and does not collect personally identifiable information. This policy will be updated if analytics are introduced. Cookies The site itself does not set any first-party cookies. Google AdSense and the Google Funding Choices CMP may set third-party cookies as described above. If you decline advertising consent, no advertising cookies will be set. Third-Party Links phq9.online links to external websites including crisis helplines, academic journals, and reference resources. These third-party sites have their own privacy policies, and the maintainer is not responsible for their content or privacy practices. GDPR (European Economic Area and UK) If you are located in the EEA or UK, the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) or its UK equivalent applies. Because phq9.online does not collect any personal data directly, the legal basis for data processing does not arise in the ordinary course of use. Advertising cookies placed by Google AdSense are subject to your explicit consent, which is collected via the Google Funding Choices CMP as described above. Google acts as a data controller for the advertising data it collects. You have the right to access, correct, or request deletion of any personal data Google holds about you via Google's My Account settings. CCPA (California Residents) If you are a California resident, the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) may apply to data collected by Google AdSense in connection with your use of this site. phq9.online does not sell your personal information. To exercise your rights under the CCPA regarding Google's data collection, please refer to Google's Privacy Policy . Changes to This Policy This privacy policy may be updated from time to time. The "last updated" date at the top of this page will reflect any changes. Continued use of the site after an update constitutes acceptance of the revised policy. Data Retention Because no personal data is collected by the maintainer, there is nothing to retain or delete on the server side. LocalStorage data on your device persists until you delete it via the history page or clear your browser's site data for phq9.online. Advertising data collected by Google is subject to Google's own data retention policies. Children phq9.online is not directed at children under the age of 13, and the site does not knowingly collect personal information from children. The screening instruments are intended for use by adults or by older adolescents under the supervision of a parent, guardian, or healthcare professional. Contact For questions about this privacy policy, contact contact@phq9.online .

---

## /promis-depression

What Is the PROMIS Depression Short Form 8a? The Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Depression Short Form 8a is an eight-item self-report measure developed by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to assess the frequency of depressive symptoms including sadness, loss of interest, feelings of worthlessness, and hopelessness over the preceding seven days. Unlike traditional depression scales that were developed through factor analysis of existing item pools, the PROMIS Depression measure was built using a modern psychometric framework called item response theory (IRT), which allows precise calibration of each item's difficulty and discrimination properties across the full severity continuum. The eight items in the Short Form 8a were selected from a much larger calibrated item bank to provide efficient, high-precision measurement of depression severity for both research and clinical monitoring purposes. The measure uses a five-point frequency scale ranging from "never" to "always." Because the items are IRT-calibrated, the Short Form 8a can be directly compared to results from the full computerised adaptive testing (CAT) version, enabling flexible deployment across different study designs and clinical contexts. History and Development The PROMIS initiative was launched by the NIH in 2004 as a large-scale effort to develop a rigorously standardised system of patient-reported outcome measures for use across clinical research and healthcare. The goal was to create measures with superior psychometric properties compared to the heterogeneous collection of legacy instruments that had accumulated across different disease areas and research traditions, making cross-study comparisons difficult. The PROMIS Depression item bank was developed and validated by Paul A. Pilkonis and colleagues at the University of Pittsburgh and across multiple PROMIS network sites. The landmark publication describing the Depression, Anxiety, and Anger item banks was published in Assessment in 2011 (doi:10.1177/1073191111411667). Item development involved systematic literature review, expert consultation, cognitive interviewing with patients, and large-scale calibration studies. The final calibrated items are available through the HealthMeasures platform maintained by the Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine. The PROMIS system is entirely in the public domain, developed with federal funding, and is freely available for use in research and clinical practice without licensing fees or permission requirements. Item Response Theory and What Makes PROMIS Different Traditional depression scales use classical test theory, treating all items as contributing equally to the total score. Item response theory takes a fundamentally different approach: each item in the PROMIS calibration is characterised by its location on the underlying severity continuum (how severe depression must be for the item to be relevant) and its discrimination (how sharply the item distinguishes between adjacent severity levels). This means that different subsets of items can be used in different settings while still producing scores that are directly comparable on a common metric. The Short Form 8a represents a fixed selection of eight items chosen to provide balanced coverage across the severity continuum. In settings where computerised adaptive testing infrastructure is available, the PROMIS CAT selects items dynamically for each patient based on their previous responses, achieving similar measurement precision with fewer items. The fixed short forms were developed to provide an IRT-based alternative in settings where adaptive testing is not feasible. Scoring and Interpretation Each of the eight items is rated on a five-point scale: 1 (never), 2 (rarely), 3 (sometimes), 4 (often), 5 (always). Raw scores range from 8 to 40. In research and clinical applications, raw scores are typically converted to T-scores using the PROMIS scoring tables derived from the IRT calibration. The T-score metric is scaled to a mean of 50 and standard deviation of 10 in the US general population, meaning a T-score of 50 represents average depressive symptom severity in the population. For the Short Form 8a used as a standalone screening tool without T-score conversion, raw score bands have been established in clinical studies. Scores in the lower range (8–11) indicate minimal depressive symptoms comparable to the general population average. Scores of 12–18 suggest mild symptoms, 19–26 moderate symptoms, and 27 and above indicate severe depressive symptomatology. These bands are approximate, and clinical interpretation should consider the full clinical context. Populations and Validation The PROMIS Depression measure has been validated across a wide range of clinical and community populations including adults with chronic conditions such as diabetes, heart disease, cancer, and HIV; adults in primary care; older adults; and community samples from diverse ethnic and socioeconomic backgrounds. Cross-cultural validation has demonstrated good measurement invariance across racial and ethnic groups in the United States, and translations have been validated in multiple languages including Spanish. The measure has also been used in paediatric research, with a separate PROMIS Pediatric Depression measure available for children aged 8 and above. In oncology research, PROMIS has become increasingly standard because of its ability to compare outcomes across different cancer types and treatment regimens on a common scale. Strengths The principal advantage of PROMIS Depression over legacy scales is the rigorous IRT-based development that ensures each item contributes meaningfully and in a characterised way to the total measurement. The T-score metric allows direct comparison of scores across studies and populations, which is particularly valuable for multi-site clinical trials and systematic reviews. Because the item bank is large and calibrated, researchers can administer different subsets of items across different timepoints or settings and still pool results with confidence. PROMIS measures have also been designed to be efficient: the eight-item short form achieves good precision in approximately two to three minutes, comparable to longer classical test instruments. The common metric across PROMIS health domains (depression, anxiety, fatigue, pain, physical function, and others) enables profiling of multiple domains simultaneously without the ceiling and floor effects that affect many legacy scales in the mild and severe ranges. Limitations The PROMIS scoring system, particularly T-score conversion, requires access to scoring tables or software, which adds a technical step compared to the simple sum scores of the PHQ-9 or GAD-7. For routine clinical use without research infrastructure, this can be a practical barrier. The one-week recall window differs from the two-week windows of the PHQ-9 and GAD-7, which means direct numerical comparison between measures requires caution. PROMIS was calibrated primarily in English-speaking US populations, and while cross-cultural validation studies have been conducted, the normative T-score framework may not be equally applicable in non-US populations where the general population distribution of depressive symptoms differs from US norms. Users in other countries should be aware that a T-score of 50 reflects the US average, not a local population average. Relationship to Other Instruments PROMIS Depression and the PHQ-9 measure related but not identical constructs: the PHQ-9 is aligned to DSM diagnostic criteria with a two-week recall window, while PROMIS Depression focuses on symptom frequency with a one-week window and uses IRT-calibrated items rather than a simple frequency count. Both are suitable for monitoring treatment response over time. Crosswalk tables exist that allow approximate conversion between PROMIS T-scores and PHQ-9 scores, enabling researchers to compare results across studies using different instruments. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician A Short Form 8a raw score of 19 or above, or a T-score of approximately 60 or above, suggests moderate-to-severe depressive symptomatology and warrants discussion with a healthcare professional. As with all self-report measures, the score should be considered in the context of duration, functional impact, and any safety concerns. If you are experiencing thoughts of self-harm, contact a clinician or crisis service immediately. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The PROMIS Depression Short Form 8a is a validated self-report measure developed by the NIH. Scores indicate symptom frequency and severity but cannot establish a clinical diagnosis of major depressive disorder or any other condition. A comprehensive clinical assessment by a qualified mental health professional is required for diagnosis and treatment planning.

---

## /scoring

Scoring Guide This page explains how each of the seven validated screening instruments available on phq9.online is scored, what the score bands mean, and what thresholds are used to distinguish severity levels. Scoring is calculated automatically when you complete a screener, but understanding the underlying method helps you interpret results in context. All scores generated here are purely for informational purposes. A score is a starting point for a conversation with a qualified clinician, not a diagnosis. See the disclaimer for full details. PHQ-9 — Patient Health Questionnaire (9 items) The PHQ-9 consists of nine items, each rated on a four-point frequency scale. Responses are summed to produce a total score ranging from 0 to 27. Higher scores indicate greater severity. 0: Not at all 1: Several days 2: More than half the days 3: Nearly every day Severity bands (Kroenke, Spitzer, Williams, 2001): 0–4: Minimal or no depression 5–9: Mild depression 10–14: Moderate depression 15–19: Moderately severe depression 20–27: Severe depression A score of 10 or greater has demonstrated sensitivity and specificity of approximately 88% each for detecting major depressive disorder in primary care settings, based on a study of 6,000 patients validated against structured psychiatric interviews. PHQ-2 — Patient Health Questionnaire (2 items) The PHQ-2 uses only the first two items of the PHQ-9 (depressed mood and anhedonia). Each item is rated 0 to 3 on the same frequency scale. The total score ranges from 0 to 6. 0–2: Screen negative — no further action required on the basis of this screen alone 3–6: Screen positive — administration of the full PHQ-9 is recommended At a cut-point of 3, sensitivity is approximately 83% and specificity approximately 92% for major depressive disorder (Kroenke, Spitzer, Williams, 2003). The PHQ-2 is intended as an ultra-brief gateway screen, not as a standalone assessment. A positive result does not indicate a diagnosis; it indicates that a more thorough evaluation is warranted. GAD-7 — Generalised Anxiety Disorder Scale (7 items) The GAD-7 assesses symptoms of generalised anxiety disorder over the preceding two weeks. Each of its seven items is rated 0 to 3 on the same frequency scale as the PHQ-9. The total score ranges from 0 to 21. 0–4: Minimal anxiety 5–9: Mild anxiety 10–14: Moderate anxiety 15–21: Severe anxiety A cut-point of 10 or greater is recommended for detecting generalised anxiety disorder, with sensitivity of 89% and specificity of 82% in the original validation study (Spitzer, Kroenke, Williams, Löwe, 2006, doi: 10.1001/archinte.166.10.1092 ). The GAD-7 is also used as a dimensional measure across anxiety disorders more broadly, including panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, and post-traumatic stress disorder. CES-D — Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (20 items) The CES-D was developed at the National Institute of Mental Health for use in epidemiological research. It contains 20 items covering depressed mood, somatic symptoms, positive affect, and interpersonal problems over the past week. Items are rated on a frequency scale: 0: Rarely or none of the time (less than 1 day) 1: Some or a little of the time (1–2 days) 2: Occasionally or a moderate amount of time (3–4 days) 3: Most or all of the time (5–7 days) Four items (numbers 4, 8, 12, and 16 in the original scale) assess positive affect and are reverse-scored: a rating of 3 contributes 0 to the sum, and a rating of 0 contributes 3. The total score ranges from 0 to 60. Severity bands: 0–15: Below clinical threshold — screen negative 16–60: At or above clinical threshold — screen positive The standard cut-point of 16 was established in the original validation (Radloff, 1977, doi: 10.1177/014662167700100306 ). The CES-D is particularly sensitive to subclinical depressive symptoms and is used extensively in longitudinal community studies. PROMIS Depression — NIH PROMIS Short Form 8a (8 items) The PROMIS Depression Short Form 8a consists of eight items drawn from the NIH Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System item bank, calibrated using item response theory. Each item is rated on a five-point frequency scale over the past seven days: 1: Never 2: Rarely 3: Sometimes 4: Often 5: Always The raw score (sum of all eight items) ranges from 8 to 40. Severity bands used on phq9.online correspond to approximate T-score ranges relative to the US general population normative sample (mean T-score of 50, SD of 10): 8–11: No or minimal symptoms (approximate T-score below 55) 12–18: Mild symptoms (approximate T-score 55–65) 19–26: Moderate symptoms (approximate T-score 65–75) 27–40: Severe symptoms (approximate T-score above 75) The PROMIS Depression instrument is in the public domain, developed with NIH funding. For precise T-score conversion, the HealthMeasures Scoring Service is available at healthmeasures.net . The band labels above are approximations suitable for consumer screening; clinical use should reference the full T-score tables. WSAS — Work and Social Adjustment Scale (5 items) The WSAS measures functional impairment across five life domains: work or ability to work, home management, social leisure activities, private leisure activities, and close personal relationships. Each domain is rated on a nine-point scale: 0: Not at all impaired 8: Very severely impaired The total score ranges from 0 to 40. Severity bands (Mundt, Marks, Shear, Greist, 2002, doi: 10.1192/bjp.180.5.461 ): 0: No impairment 1–9: Subclinical impairment — mild functional limitation that may not require specific intervention 10–19: Significant impairment associated with notable distress or disability 20–40: Severe impairment — clinical-level functional disability warranting assessment and treatment The WSAS is often used alongside disorder-specific screeners such as the PHQ-9 or GAD-7 to assess whether depressive or anxiety symptoms are associated with meaningful functional impairment. It is not itself a diagnostic instrument. WHO-5 — WHO Well-Being Index (5 items) The WHO-5 assesses subjective psychological wellbeing over the past two weeks using five positive statements. Each item is rated on a six-point scale: 0: At no time 1: Some of the time 2: Less than half of the time 3: More than half of the time 4: Most of the time 5: All of the time The raw score (sum of items, 0–25) is multiplied by 4 to produce a percentage score on a scale of 0 to 100. Higher scores indicate better wellbeing. Severity bands: 0–28: Very low wellbeing — likely depression; further assessment is recommended 29–50: Low wellbeing — possible depression; monitoring or follow-up is warranted 51–100: Normal range wellbeing A WHO-5 score below 50 is used as a threshold for further assessment in the WHO's own guidance and has been incorporated into diabetes care guidelines. The WHO-5 assesses positive wellbeing rather than negative symptoms, which makes it complementary to depression-focused instruments. It is particularly useful for monitoring improvement over time rather than measuring symptom severity in the traditional sense. How to Use These Scores Scores from any of these instruments should be interpreted in the context of the individual's full clinical picture, including medical history, current stressors, functioning, and any other information a clinician has gathered. No cut-point is a clinical decision rule. These instruments assist in decision-making but do not replace clinical judgment. For guidance on what different severity levels mean clinically and when to seek professional support, see the interpretation guide . For crisis contacts if you are in distress now, see the crisis resources page.

---

## /who-5

What Is the WHO-5 Well-Being Index? The World Health Organization-Five Well-Being Index (WHO-5) is a five-item self-report questionnaire that measures subjective psychological wellbeing over the preceding two weeks. Unlike the PHQ-9 or GAD-7, which ask about the presence and frequency of negative symptoms, the WHO-5 is framed entirely in positive terms: respondents rate how often they have felt cheerful and in good spirits, calm and relaxed, active and vigorous, woke up feeling fresh and rested, and found their daily life filled with things of interest. This positive framing makes the WHO-5 one of the few widely validated measures of wellbeing rather than ill-being, placing it at a distinct point on the mental health assessment landscape. The WHO-5 has been used as a screening instrument for depression, as a measure of general psychological wellbeing, as an outcome measure in clinical trials and public health interventions, and as a population monitoring tool in national health surveys. Its brevity, positive framing, and free availability have contributed to widespread adoption across primary care systems in Europe, North America, and Asia-Pacific. History and Development The WHO-5 was developed by the World Health Organization Regional Office for Europe. The current 1998 version was adapted from the WHO-10 Well-Being Index, which itself was derived from the WHO-BREF quality of life assessment. The five-item version was designed to retain the psychometric properties of the longer instrument while reducing respondent burden to the minimum practical for routine primary care screening. The WHO-5 was recommended by the WHO Regional Office for Europe as a screening instrument for depression in primary care settings. It gained particular prominence as part of the WHO's Audit Project Odense (APO) — a multicentre quality improvement programme in European primary care — and through its use in large population surveys in Denmark, Iceland, the United Kingdom, and other European countries. The Danish Psychiatric Centre in Copenhagen maintains an official WHO-5 resource website and has coordinated many of the translation and validation efforts. The WHO-5 is available free of charge for use in research and clinical practice, with the stipulation that attribution to the World Health Organization is provided. The 0 to 100 Scale Each of the five items is rated on a six-point scale: 0 (at no time), 1 (some of the time), 2 (less than half of the time), 3 (more than half of the time), 4 (most of the time), 5 (all of the time). The raw scores for each item are summed to produce a raw total between 0 and 25. This raw score is then multiplied by 4 to produce a percentage score on a 0 to 100 scale, where 0 represents the worst imaginable wellbeing (having experienced no positive feelings at all during the past two weeks) and 100 represents the best imaginable wellbeing (having experienced all positive feelings all of the time throughout the past two weeks). This 0-to-100 transformation makes the score intuitively interpretable and facilitates communication with patients. A score of 50, for example, represents a person who experienced positive wellbeing about half of the time, which provides a readily understandable reference point. The percentage format also makes it straightforward to track change over time and communicate progress in therapeutic settings. Interpretation and Severity Bands The primary clinical cut-point for the WHO-5 as a depression screening tool is a score below 50, which indicates reduced wellbeing and warrants further evaluation. A score of 28 or below (raw score of 7 or below) suggests a likelihood of depression and is recommended as a threshold for proceeding to a more detailed assessment such as the PHQ-9. 0–28: Very low wellbeing. Suggests a likelihood of clinical depression or significant distress. Further evaluation with a validated depression scale and clinical assessment is recommended. 29–50: Reduced wellbeing. Below the threshold that suggests probable depression, but indicates that positive experiences are relatively infrequent. Monitoring and discussion with a clinician may be appropriate depending on context and trend. 51–100: Normal to high wellbeing. Scores in this range indicate that positive wellbeing is experienced more often than not. High scores do not rule out specific mental health conditions, but suggest that overall subjective wellbeing is relatively preserved. Psychometric Properties The WHO-5 has been extensively evaluated across dozens of studies. A systematic review and meta-analysis published by Topp and colleagues in 2015 pooled results across studies and reported that using a cut-point of 50 or below, the WHO-5 had a sensitivity of approximately 0.85 and specificity of approximately 0.73 for major depressive disorder. These properties are broadly comparable to other brief depression screens, though the WHO-5's positive framing means it captures a somewhat different aspect of the mood disturbance than symptom-focused instruments. Internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) is typically reported between 0.82 and 0.89. The factor structure is consistently unidimensional, meaning all five items tap a single latent dimension of wellbeing rather than distinct subscales. This unidimensional structure supports the use of the total score as a summary measure without subscale scoring. Positive Framing and Its Implications The entirely positive framing of the WHO-5 items is both a strength and a distinctive feature that differentiates it from other instruments. Positive wellbeing and the absence of depressive symptoms are related but not identical constructs. A person may have low WHO-5 scores (infrequent positive experiences) without meeting criteria for major depression, or may score moderately on the WHO-5 while still experiencing significant depressive symptoms on the PHQ-9. This conceptual distinction is consistent with the two-continua model of mental health, which holds that mental illness and mental wellbeing are not opposite ends of a single spectrum but partially independent dimensions. For clinical purposes, this means the WHO-5 adds information beyond symptom scales: it captures the presence of positive experience, which is itself therapeutically meaningful. Treatment goals in positive psychology interventions, wellbeing therapy, and some CBT approaches include not just the reduction of negative symptoms but the cultivation of positive experiences, and the WHO-5 can track progress on this dimension specifically. Population Use and Cross-Cultural Validation The WHO-5 has been translated into over 30 languages and validated in primary care and community populations across Europe, North America, Africa, and Asia. It is widely used in European national health surveys, including in Denmark, Iceland, and Germany, where it is part of routine population monitoring. In the United Kingdom, it has been used in general practice quality improvement initiatives and patient experience surveys. In diabetes care, the WHO-5 has become particularly established as a monitoring tool, with the WHO recommending its use for detecting emotional problems in people with diabetes. Cross-cultural validation has generally supported measurement invariance across diverse populations, although some studies have found differential item functioning on specific items in non-European samples, suggesting that local normative data and cultural adaptation may improve interpretation in some contexts. Strengths The WHO-5 is extremely brief, requiring approximately one minute to complete, and its positive framing is generally experienced as less distressing to answer than symptom-focused questionnaires. For patients who are reluctant to describe negative symptoms in detail, starting with a positive-framing instrument can lower barriers to initial engagement with mental health screening. The instrument is well validated and internationally recognised, and its free availability under WHO attribution makes it accessible without licensing constraints. The 0-to-100 scoring range provides a psychologically intuitive metric that patients and clinicians can engage with directly. The ability to track change on this percentage scale over time — across appointments, treatment phases, or life circumstances — adds longitudinal utility beyond its screening function. Limitations Because the WHO-5 measures positive wellbeing rather than specific depressive symptoms, it has somewhat lower specificity for major depressive disorder than the PHQ-9 when used as a standalone depression screen. A low WHO-5 score indicates reduced wellbeing but does not specify the cause: physical illness, life adversity, situational stress, and depressive disorder can all produce low WHO-5 scores. This breadth is a feature for general wellbeing monitoring but a limitation for differential diagnosis. The WHO-5 does not include items assessing suicidality, hopelessness, or functional impairment, which are important clinical dimensions. It should not be used as a sole assessment tool in settings where safety assessment is a primary concern. Relationship to Other Instruments The WHO-5 is most naturally used as a complement to symptom-focused instruments rather than as a replacement. Pairing the WHO-5 with the PHQ-9 provides a picture of both negative symptoms (PHQ-9) and positive wellbeing (WHO-5), capturing a fuller dimensional profile of mental health. The WHO-5 can also be paired with the WSAS to add information about functional consequences. In primary care settings where time is short, the WHO-5 provides a quick, non-stigmatising entry point into mental health monitoring that can be followed up with more detailed screening when scores are low. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician A WHO-5 score below 50 warrants a conversation with a healthcare professional, particularly if the low score has been persistent over several assessments or is accompanied by other concerns. A score below 28 specifically suggests that further evaluation for depression is warranted. If you are experiencing persistent low mood, loss of interest, or any thoughts of self-harm, please discuss this with a clinician regardless of your WHO-5 score. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The WHO-5 Well-Being Index is a validated measure of subjective psychological wellbeing. A low score indicates reduced wellbeing and may suggest the presence of depression or another condition warranting further evaluation, but it does not constitute a clinical diagnosis. Only a qualified clinician can assess your mental health comprehensively and advise on appropriate support or treatment.

---

## /wsas

What Is the WSAS? The Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS) is a five-item self-report measure that quantifies the degree to which a psychological problem is interfering with a person's ability to function across five key life domains: ability to work, ability to manage home, social leisure activities, private leisure activities, and close relationships. Each item is rated on a nine-point scale from 0 (no impairment at all) to 8 (very severely impairs and I have stopped doing this because of my problem). The WSAS was designed not to measure symptom severity directly but to capture the functional consequences of any psychological condition, making it agnostic to the specific diagnosis or symptom type. This symptom-agnostic design is one of the WSAS's most important features. Because it measures functional disability rather than specific symptoms, it can be paired with any disease-specific measure — depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, or others — to provide a complementary picture of how much the condition is actually affecting daily life. A high PHQ-9 score paired with a high WSAS score indicates both significant symptoms and significant impairment; a high PHQ-9 paired with a low WSAS may indicate that, despite depressive symptoms, the person is maintaining everyday function and the urgency for intervention may differ. History and Development The WSAS was developed by James C. Mundt, Isaac M. Marks, M. Katherine Shear, and John H. Greist and was first described in a paper published in the British Journal of Psychiatry in 2002 (doi:10.1192/bjp.180.5.461). The scale's origins trace back to work by Isaac Marks at the Maudsley Hospital in London, where simple functional impairment ratings were used clinically to complement symptom measures in anxiety and phobia research from the 1970s onwards. The WSAS formalized and extended these clinical ratings into a validated psychometric instrument. The 2002 validation study demonstrated that the WSAS was sensitive to treatment change and that it discriminated meaningfully between levels of symptomatic severity across different anxiety and depressive disorders. The instrument was deliberately kept brief — five items rated on a nine-point scale — to maximise compliance and minimise respondent burden in both research and routine clinical monitoring contexts. The WSAS is in the public domain and may be freely used, copied, and translated. It is included in the NHS IAPT minimum dataset in England, meaning it is collected for every patient at every session in all IAPT services alongside the PHQ-9 and GAD-7. Psychometric Properties The original validation by Mundt et al. reported high internal consistency, with Cronbach's alpha of 0.89 across multiple diagnostic groups including depression, panic disorder, agoraphobia, OCD, and social phobia. The WSAS demonstrated good convergent validity, correlating strongly with global clinician-rated functional assessments and with the severity of primary symptom measures across different conditions. The instrument showed good sensitivity to change over the course of treatment. In studies of cognitive behavioural therapy and pharmacotherapy for anxiety and depression, WSAS scores declined significantly in parallel with symptom improvement, providing evidence that the scale measures a construct related to but distinct from symptom severity itself — namely, the functional consequences of those symptoms in everyday life. Test-retest reliability has been reported at approximately 0.73 in stable patients, which is adequate for a measure intended to capture change over time. The scale has demonstrated discriminant validity between diagnostic groups of different severity levels. Scoring and Interpretation Each of the five items is rated on a scale from 0 to 8, giving a total score range of 0 to 40. Higher scores indicate greater functional impairment. The scoring conventions established in the literature are: 0–9: Subclinical impairment. Functional disruption at this level is unlikely to represent a clinically significant condition, though monitoring is appropriate if symptoms are new or worsening. 10–19: Significant functional impairment, typically associated with a diagnosable psychiatric condition causing moderate disruption to daily life. Treatment is usually appropriate. 20–40: Severe functional impairment. Scores in this range are associated with severe psychiatric disorder and indicate a substantial impact on the ability to work, maintain relationships, and participate in ordinary social activities. In NHS IAPT recovery criteria, functional recovery is defined as a WSAS score below 10 at the end of treatment. This dual requirement — achieving both symptom recovery (PHQ-9 below 10 and GAD-7 below 8) and functional recovery (WSAS below 10) — represents a more demanding and arguably more clinically meaningful definition of treatment success than symptom scores alone. The Relationship Between Functional Impairment and Symptom Severity An important insight from WSAS research is that symptom severity and functional impairment, while correlated, are not the same thing. Two people with identical PHQ-9 scores of 15 may have WSAS scores that differ by 20 points, depending on their coping resources, social support, nature of their employment, and other contextual factors. This dissociation means that treatment outcomes should ideally be assessed on both dimensions independently. Some patients achieve substantial symptomatic improvement (large PHQ-9 reductions) with modest functional gains, while others show the opposite pattern. Understanding these trajectories is clinically informative and helps guide decisions about whether additional interventions focused specifically on functional rehabilitation — such as supported employment, occupational therapy, or graded activity programmes — may be warranted beyond symptom-focused psychological therapy. NHS IAPT and Routine Outcome Monitoring The inclusion of the WSAS in the NHS IAPT minimum dataset reflects a policy decision to measure treatment success beyond symptom reduction alone. IAPT services in England collect PHQ-9, GAD-7, and WSAS scores at every appointment for every patient, generating one of the most comprehensive routine outcome monitoring datasets in the world. Published IAPT annual reports use these data to report on recovery rates, reliable improvement rates, and deterioration rates at service and national level, enabling benchmarking and quality improvement across hundreds of individual services. Strengths The WSAS is extremely brief, requiring approximately one to two minutes to complete, yet it captures five distinct life domains that are meaningful to patients and clinicians. Its symptom-agnostic design means it can be used across diagnostic categories without modification, making it valuable in routine care settings where patients present with heterogeneous conditions. The nine-point response format provides more granularity than four-point scales, which may improve sensitivity to change over time. Limitations Item 1 (work) may be difficult to interpret for people who are retired, unemployed, or unable to work for reasons unrelated to their mental health. In these cases, respondents are typically instructed to rate how their problem affects their ability to do housework or other regular activities in place of paid employment. This adaptation is not always clearly explained in standard administration, which can introduce ambiguity. The WSAS does not specify what "the problem" is, which means respondents must hold in mind the correct referent condition when rating each item. In patients with multiple comorbidities, it may be unclear whether they are rating impairment due to depression, anxiety, physical illness, or some combination, which can complicate interpretation. When to Discuss Results With a Clinician A WSAS score of 10 or above suggests that a psychological condition is causing significant interference with everyday functioning and warrants discussion with a healthcare professional. Even lower scores are informative context for understanding the impact of any symptoms you are experiencing. Discussing your WSAS score alongside your PHQ-9 and GAD-7 results gives your clinician a more complete picture of both your symptom burden and its real-world consequences. This Tool Is Screening, Not Diagnosis The WSAS is a validated measure of functional impairment. It does not assess symptoms or diagnose any mental health condition. A high WSAS score indicates that something is significantly affecting your day-to-day functioning and that a clinical evaluation is warranted to identify the cause and discuss appropriate support.
